stage.name: Security & Wallets
stage.description: Cryptographic key derivation (BIP39/44), wallet architectures, attack vectors, multisignature schemes, and operational security.

generatedAgentConfigs.name: Dr. Kim
generatedAgentConfigs.persona: A cryptographer and security researcher specializing in key management and adversarial analysis.

elements.content: Key Derivation — BIP39 & BIP44 Standards
How 12 words become billions of private keys

2¹²⁸
12-word entropy

2048 iters
PBKDF2

BIP39 — Mnemonic Phrase

  • 128 bits entropy → 12 words. 256 bits → 24 words
  • 2048-word wordlist (English). 12 words = 2¹²⁸ combinations
  • Passphrase (optional): PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512, 2048 iterations
  • Output: 512-bit seed — derived deterministically from words
  • BIP39 is NOT encrypted — anyone with words controls funds

BIP44 — HD Wallet Paths

  • Hierarchical Deterministic: 1 seed → unlimited keys
  • Path: m / purpose' / coin_type' / account' / change / index
  • Bitcoin: m/44'/0'/0'/0/0. Ethereum: m/44'/60'/0'/0/0
  • XRPL: m/44'/144'/0'/0/0. Single seed → all chains
  • Hardened derivation (') prevents child key compromise

The Seed → Key Journey

  • Entropy → mnemonic → seed (PBKDF2) → master key (HMAC-SHA512)
  • Master key splits: 256-bit private key + 256-bit chain code
  • CKD function derives child keys from parent + chain code + index
  • Extended public key (xpub): watch-only wallet — no spend access
  • Never store seed digitally — steel plate backup recommended
Wallet Architecture Types
Custodial vs self-custody — the security/convenience tradeoff

$1.5B+
CEX hacks

$50-250
HW wallet

Custodial Wallets (CEX)

  • Exchange holds your private keys — you trust the institution
  • Convenient: easy UI, fiat on-ramp, customer support
  • Risk: exchange hacks ($1.5B+ stolen from CEXs historically)
  • Mt. Gox 2014: 850k BTC lost. FTX 2022: $8B in customer funds
  • Not your keys, not your coins — Rule #1 of crypto

Hot Wallets (Self-Custody)

  • Private keys on internet-connected device — mobile or desktop
  • MetaMask, Xaman (XRPL), Trust Wallet — free, convenient
  • Risk: malware, phishing, clipboard hijacking attacks
  • Best practice: only keep spending money in hot wallet
  • Never import seed phrase into any website — EVER

Cold Storage (Hardware Wallets)

  • Private keys stored offline in secure element chip
  • Ledger, Trezor, Keystone — sign txs offline, broadcast online
  • Cost: $50-250. Worth it for >$1k in crypto
  • Air-gapped: never connects to internet (Keystone, Coldcard)
  • Supply chain attack: buy ONLY from official manufacturer
Attack Vectors — Threat Modeling
Know your enemy before they know your seed phrase

$300M+
Approval scams

SIM swap
#1 social

Remote Attacks

  • Phishing: fake websites (metamásk.io vs metamask.io)
  • Clipboard hijacking: malware replaces copied address
  • Fake browser extension: 15+ fake MetaMask extensions found
  • Approval exploit: sign malicious transaction → drain wallet
  • Wallet drainer: $300M+ stolen via approval scams (2023)

Physical & Social Attacks

  • $5 wrench attack: physical coercion — most underrated threat
  • SIM swap: port your phone number → bypass SMS 2FA
  • Social engineering: impersonate support — never share seed
  • Evil maid: physical device access while unattended
  • Duress wallet: decoy wallet with plausible balance

Operational Security (OpSec)

  • Never photograph or type seed phrase on any device
  • Use hardware security key (YubiKey) for exchange 2FA
  • Separate identity: don't link on-chain activity to real name
  • Verify every character of destination address — twice
  • Whitelist withdrawal addresses on exchanges — extra friction = good
Multisignature Wallets
Requiring multiple keys — institutional-grade security for anyone

2-of-3
Common setup

$100B+
Gnosis Safe

M-of-N Multisig

  • Requires M signatures from N total keys to authorize a tx
  • 2-of-3: any 2 of 3 keys can sign — lose 1 key, still have access
  • 3-of-5: higher security, requires 3 keys — used by institutions
  • Bitcoin native multisig: P2SH, P2WSH. Ethereum: Gnosis Safe
  • XRPL: SignerList amendment — native multisig on ledger

Geographic Distribution

  • Store keys in different physical locations — city, country
  • Example 2-of-3: key 1=home safe, key 2=bank vault, key 3=trusted person
  • Eliminates single point of failure — fire, theft, loss
  • Gnosis Safe: ~$100B+ secured in multisig contracts
  • Time-locks: add 24-48h delay on large withdrawals

Multisig vs Seed Phrase

  • Single-sig: lose seed = lose everything. Multisig: redundancy
  • Multisig: harder to set up, more complex recovery
  • For <$10k: hardware wallet sufficient. >$50k: consider multisig
  • Shamir Secret Sharing (SSS): split key into N shares, need M
  • Never store all key shares with same person or location
XRPL Account Security Features
Advanced security primitives built into the XRPL protocol

1 XRP
Base reserve

0.2 XRP
Per object

Account Reserves & Structure

  • Base reserve: 1 XRP minimum balance per account
  • Owner reserve: 0.2 XRP per object (trust line, offer, etc.)
  • Account cannot be deleted below reserve — spam prevention
  • Destination tags: route payments to sub-accounts on exchanges
  • AccountID: 20-byte hash of public key — unique identifier

Key Management Features

  • Regular key: set alternate key pair for signing — rotate safely
  • Master key disable: lsfDisableMaster flag — use regular key only
  • SignerList: native multisig — M-of-N threshold on-ledger
  • Key rotation: change keys without moving funds
  • AccountSet flags: require destination tag, freeze accounts

Advanced Security Config

  • Disable master key after setting regular key — prevents leak exposure
  • asfRequireDest: force all payments to include destination tag
  • asfDisallowXRP: signals account not meant for direct XRP payments
  • Escrow: time-lock XRP — conditional release (great for payroll)
  • Checks: off-ledger payment orders — claim within expiry window

content.html:
⚠️ SECURITY REMINDER: Never enter your real seed phrase into any website, including educational tools. This demo uses no real cryptography — it is illustrative only.

BIP39 Seed Phrase Security Analysis

128 bits
12-word entropy
256 bits
24-word entropy
2,048
Words in BIP39 list
✅ Secure seed phrase practices
✓Generated by a verified hardware wallet (Ledger/Trezor/Coldcard) using hardware CSPRNG
✓Written on paper immediately, never typed, photographed, or stored digitally
✓Stored in a fireproof safe or engraved on steel (survives fire at 1,400°C)
✓Additional passphrase (25th word) for hidden wallet plausible deniability
✓Backup copies in 2+ geographically separate locations
❌ Fatal seed phrase mistakes
✗Typing seed phrase into MetaMask on a compromised computer
✗Storing in iCloud Notes, Google Drive, email drafts, or password managers
✗Taking a photo of the seed phrase (cloud photo backup is automatic on most phones)
✗Sharing via any digital channel — Discord DMs, Telegram, text message
✗Generating phrases from online tools (this page, or any website)
🔢 Cracking time estimates
•128-bit security (12 words): ~3.4 × 10³⁸ years at 1 trillion attempts/second — computationally impossible
•256-bit security (24 words): square of the above — utterly beyond any conceivable computer
•Weak passphrase on top: drastically reduces effective entropy — dictionary words are crackable

actions.text: The most common way people lose cryptocurrency is not through sophisticated hacking — it's through basic operational security failures. Seed phrases stored in cloud notes, photographed, or typed into phishing sites. The cryptography is unbreakable. Human behavior is the weakest link.

questions.question: BIP44 specifies a derivation path m/44'/coin_type'/account'/change/index. What coin_type value is used for XRP (XRPL)?
questions.analysis: XRPL coin_type is 144 per the SLIP-0044 registry. Bitcoin is 0, Ethereum is 60, TRON is 195. These values ensure wallets derive completely separate key trees for each blockchain from the same root seed.

questions.question: What is the primary security advantage of 'hardened' child key derivation in BIP32?

options.label: It generates longer, more secure private keys
Compromise of the parent extended public key (xpub) cannot be used to derive child private keys
It enables multisignature transactions without an additional setup
Hardened keys are encrypted with AES-256

questions.analysis: In normal derivation, knowing the parent xpub + a child private key can derive the parent private key. Hardened derivation (index ≥ 2³¹) breaks this: child keys are derived from the private key, not the public key, so xpub exposure doesn't compromise the tree.

questions.question: Which of the following are secure ways to store a BIP39 seed phrase? (Select all that apply)

options.label: Written on paper, stored in a fireproof safe
Saved in an encrypted password manager like 1Password
Engraved on a Cryptosteel or stainless steel plate
Split using Shamir Secret Sharing across 3 geographically separate locations

questions.analysis: Paper in a fireproof safe (A), metal backup (C), and Shamir sharding (D) are all appropriate. Password managers (B) are digital storage connected to cloud services — if your 1Password account or device is compromised, your seed is exposed. Seed phrases should never be stored in any digital medium.

questions.question: You're sending XRP to Coinbase and forget to include the destination tag. What happens?

options.label: The transaction is rejected and XRP returned automatically
XRP arrives at Coinbase's address but cannot be credited to your account — effectively lost
The XRP is held in escrow for 72 hours then returned
Coinbase credits the XRP based on your IP address

questions.analysis: Coinbase uses one XRP address for millions of customers. Without a destination tag, they cannot identify which customer sent the funds. The XRP arrives at their address on-chain, but Coinbase support cannot credit it without the tag — the funds become irrecoverable in practice.

questions.question: A 2-of-3 multisig wallet has keys distributed: Key 1 (home hardware wallet), Key 2 (safe deposit box), Key 3 (trusted attorney). An attacker breaks into your home and steals Key 1. What can they do?

options.label: Access all funds immediately with Key 1 alone
Nothing — they have 1 of 3 keys but need 2 to sign
Initiate a recovery process using Key 1

questions.analysis: 2-of-3 multisig requires any 2 of the 3 keys to sign. With only Key 1, the attacker cannot produce a valid transaction. This demonstrates multisig's core value: no single point of compromise. You should then use the remaining 2 keys to move funds to a new wallet and revoke the compromised key.

questions.question: Explain the BIP39 PBKDF2 process: starting from the mnemonic phrase, describe each step that produces the final HD wallet seed. Include the role of an optional passphrase and why 2,048 PBKDF2 iterations are used.
questions.commentPrompt: Full marks for: (1) mnemonic words as input, (2) PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512 with 'mnemonic' + optional_passphrase as salt, (3) 2048 iterations to slow brute-force while remaining usable, (4) output is 512-bit seed, (5) optional passphrase creates entirely separate wallet tree — same mnemonic, different seed if passphrase differs, enabling plausible deniability.
